main_damage_causes: Complete absence from GoF film removes the entire foundation of Percy's arc - his blind Crouch loyalty, his 'Weatherby' humiliation, and his first steps toward the Ministry over family - making his OotP estrangement unmotivated for film audiences

The OotP estrangement arc (letter to Ron, public betrayal, Christmas jumper returned, Mrs. Weasley's grief) is entirely unfilmed, meaning Percy's DH reconciliation and Fred's death carry none of their intended emotional weight

Systematic removal of humanizing moments (Penelope subplot, Christmas scenes, sleeping-bag night, N.E.W.T. stress) reduces Percy to a one-note pompous background figure rather than a character whose flaws are understandable and whose fall is tragic

damaging_scenes.scene: PS Film Scene 1 - King's Cross introduction
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Percy marches confidently through the barrier first, already in Hogwarts robes with prefect badge, establishing his pompous pride in his status from the very first appearance.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Percy passes through the barrier as part of a group rush with Fred and George, no robes, no badge, no distinguishing characterization. He is visually indistinguishable from his brothers.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The book uses this first appearance to immediately establish Percy's pomposity and pride in his prefect status. The film collapses him into the background.

damaging_scenes.scene: PS Film Scene 2 - Sorting feast, Percy as information source
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Percy answers Harry's questions about Snape and Dumbledore with authority and airiness ('He's a genius! Best wizard in the world! But he is a bit mad, yes'), establishing him as a pompous but knowledgeable presence who takes Harry under his wing at the feast.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Percy answers Harry's question about Snape briefly and functionally ('Oh, that's Professor Snape, head of Slytherin house... Potions. But everyone knows it's the Dark Arts he fancies'). The characteristic airy pomposity and the warm 'Potatoes, Harry?' moment are absent.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The book's Percy is self-important but also genuinely helpful and engaged with Harry. The film reduces this to a functional exposition delivery, losing the voice.

damaging_scenes.scene: PS Film Scene 3 - Leading first years to dormitory
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Percy leads the first years with authority, faces down Peeves by threatening the Bloody Baron, demonstrates competence and command ('Go away, Peeves, or the Baron'll hear about this, I mean it!'), and gives useful warnings about Peeves.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Percy leads the students with generic instructions ('Keep up, please', 'This is the most direct path'). The entire Peeves confrontation is absent. Percy shows no initiative or courage, just bureaucratic shepherding.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The Peeves scene is one of Percy's best early moments - he shows genuine authority and protects the first years. Its removal strips him of a defining competence beat.

damaging_scenes.scene: PS Film Scene 5 - Gryffindor common room arrival
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Percy gives the password, directs students to dormitories, and takes charge of the common room with prefect authority.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Percy gives the password and directs students, which is retained, but the scene is compressed and his authority is minimal.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Minor compression but the pompous prefect voice is largely absent.

damaging_scenes.scene: PS Film - Absent: Percy pompously congratulating Ron at end-of-year feast
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Percy is heard telling other prefects 'My brother, you know! My youngest brother! Got past McGonagall's giant chess set!' - showing family pride beneath the pomposity, a humanizing and comic moment.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: This moment is entirely absent. Percy has no presence at the end-of-year feast.
damaging_scenes.explanation: This is one of the few moments showing Percy's genuine family feeling and pride in Ron, humanizing him beyond pure pomposity. Its loss removes a key dimension of the sibling relationship.

damaging_scenes.scene: PS Film - Absent: Christmas scenes (sweater, chess, prefect badge chase)
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Fred and George force Percy's sweater over his head and frog-march him to Christmas dinner; Percy nearly breaks his teeth on a sickle; Percy tries to help Harry at chess; Percy chases Fred and George for stealing his prefect badge. These scenes establish Percy as a full family member, pompous but integrated.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Percy has no presence in the PS Christmas sequences.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The Christmas scenes are the richest depiction of Percy within the Weasley family dynamic - his pomposity teased but his belonging affirmed. Their complete absence removes the foundation for understanding his later estrangement as a tragedy.

damaging_scenes.scene: CoS Film Scene 7 - Burrow arrival, Percy absent from family dynamic
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Percy is present at the Burrow, already dressed with prefect badge, acting pompously about his exam results and shutting himself in his room. Ron notes 'Percy's been acting very oddly this summer.' This foreshadows his secretive nature around Penelope.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Percy appears briefly only to demonstrate Floo Powder for Harry ('Certainly, Mother. Don't worry, Harry. It's simple enough'). He is helpful and pleasant with no hint of the odd secretive behavior or pomposity noted in the book.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The film uses Percy purely as a functional demonstration device, losing the characterization of his secretive summer behavior.

damaging_scenes.scene: CoS Film - Absent: Knockturn Alley/Diagon Alley Percy scenes
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Percy is found in a junk shop reading 'Prefects Who Gained Power' ('A study of Hogwarts prefects and their later careers'). Ron tells Harry Percy wants to be Minister of Magic. This is a key characterization of his ambition.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Percy has no presence in the Diagon Alley sequence beyond the Floo Powder scene.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The 'Prefects Who Gained Power' moment is one of the most efficient characterizations of Percy's ambition in the entire series. Its absence means the film never establishes his ministerial ambitions, making his later career choices less legible.

damaging_scenes.scene: CoS Film Scene 8 - King's Cross, Percy goes through barrier
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Percy strides briskly forward and vanishes through the barrier first, then is seen on the platform walking with chest thrown out to show Penelope his badge. The Penelope relationship is introduced here with Percy going pink and Ginny and Harry sharing a laugh.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Percy, Fred and George stride through the barrier together in a group. The Penelope moment on the platform is absent.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The Penelope introduction is important for the CoS mystery subplot (her petrification affecting Percy) and humanizes Percy with a romantic relationship. The film introduces Penelope only in the lunch scene with no emotional setup.

damaging_scenes.scene: CoS Film Scene 9 - Polyjuice corridor encounter
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Percy is wandering the dungeons suspiciously at Christmas (secretly meeting Penelope), confronts Harry-as-Crabbe and Ron-as-Goyle, and is then dismissed by Malfoy ('That Peter Weasley'). Percy's mysterious behavior is part of the running subplot about what he's hiding.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Percy appears in the corridor and confronts the disguised Harry and Ron. His presence is explained only as 'I happen to be a prefect' with no hint of his own suspicious reason for being there. The mystery of what Percy is doing is absent.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The book's Percy in this scene is himself acting suspiciously, which is part of the Penelope subplot. The film makes him a simple obstacle with no irony.

damaging_scenes.scene: CoS Film - Absent: Percy pale and stunned after Penelope's petrification
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Percy is described as 'pale and stunned' after Penelope Clearwater is petrified. George tells Harry quietly 'Percy's in shock... He didn't think the monster would dare attack a prefect.' This shows Percy's vulnerability and genuine feeling.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Penelope Clearwater is not petrified in the film. Percy's emotional response to her attack is entirely absent.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Penelope's petrification and Percy's devastated reaction is one of the most humanizing moments for Percy in CoS, showing real emotional depth beneath the pomposity. Its absence (along with the entire Penelope subplot resolution) means Percy's romantic life and vulnerability are never shown.

damaging_scenes.scene: CoS Film - Absent: Percy interrupting Ginny's confession, the secret revealed
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Percy interrupts just as Ginny is about to confess about the Chamber, causing Ron to be furious. Later it's revealed Percy was hiding his relationship with Penelope - 'I walked in on them kissing in an empty classroom.' The resolution is comic and touching.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: This entire subplot - Percy's secret, Ginny's interrupted confession, the revelation about Penelope - is absent from the film.
damaging_scenes.explanation: This subplot gives Percy a genuine narrative function in CoS (his secret inadvertently delays Ginny's confession) and resolves with a humanizing reveal. Its absence removes Percy's only plot-relevant role in the second film.

damaging_scenes.scene: CoS Film - Absent: Percy's reaction to Ginny's taking
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: When Ginny is taken, Percy shuts himself in his dormitory and goes to send an owl to his parents. He is devastated. This shows genuine family feeling.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Percy has no visible reaction to Ginny's abduction.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Percy's grief at Ginny's taking is a key humanizing moment showing his love for his family beneath the pomposity.

damaging_scenes.scene: PoA Film Scene 10 - Leaky Cauldron reunion
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Percy holds out his hand 'solemnly as though he and Harry had never met' and says 'Harry. How nice to see you... I hope you're well?' - pompous but affectionate in a comic way. Fred elbows him aside. Percy scowls. He is fully integrated into the family comedy.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Percy appears in the group but has no dialogue or distinguishing characterization in this scene.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The book's PoA reunion is one of Percy's funniest moments. The film gives him no presence.

damaging_scenes.scene: PoA Film Scene 11 - Slashed Fat Lady portrait
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Percy takes charge authoritatively ('Back! All of you! No one is to enter this dormitory until it has been fully searched!'), then later has a substantive conversation with Dumbledore about the Fat Lady and the dementors, showing his genuine engagement with school safety.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Percy pushes through the crowd saying 'Let me through, please. Excuse me, thank you, I'm Head Boy...' then 'Back! All of you!' This is retained but his subsequent role managing the sleeping-bag night and his conversation with Dumbledore is absent.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The book gives Percy a substantial role managing the crisis night - prowling between sleeping bags, reporting to Dumbledore, asking about the Fat Lady. The film reduces him to a brief crowd-control moment.

damaging_scenes.scene: PoA Film - Absent: Percy managing the sleeping-bag night and Dumbledore conversation
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Percy is left in charge of the students overnight, prowls between sleeping bags enforcing quiet, has a private conversation with Dumbledore about the Fat Lady and dementors, and is described as 'rapt with attention.' This is his most substantial responsible action in the series.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: This entire sequence is absent from the film.
damaging_scenes.explanation: This is Percy's finest hour as Head Boy - trusted by Dumbledore, competent, responsible. Its absence means the film never shows Percy actually performing his role well, making him purely comic rather than a character whose later fall from grace carries weight.

damaging_scenes.scene: PoA Film - Absent: Percy's N.E.W.T. stress and Ministry ambitions
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Percy is described as increasingly edgy preparing for N.E.W.T.s, hoping to enter the Ministry of Magic, needing top grades. He gets top-grade N.E.W.T.s. Ron notes Percy 'wouldn't come home if Dad didn't make him' once he starts at the Ministry.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Percy's academic achievement and Ministry ambitions are never established in the films.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Percy's ambition and his Ministry career are the foundation of his entire arc in books 4-5. Without establishing this in PoA, his later choices lack context.

damaging_scenes.scene: GoF Film - Absent entirely
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: GoF contains Percy's richest material: his pompous Ministry work on cauldron thickness, his hero-worship of Barty Crouch (who calls him 'Weatherby'), his promotion to Crouch's personal assistant, his defense of Crouch's treatment of Winky, his role at the Quidditch World Cup, his presence at the Yule Ball, his letter to Ron advising him to drop Harry, and his being hauled in for questioning after Crouch's disappearance.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Percy is entirely absent from the GoF film.
damaging_scenes.explanation: GoF is the pivotal book for Percy's arc. His blind loyalty to Crouch, his ambition overriding his judgment, his 'Weatherby' humiliation, and his being implicated in Crouch's disappearance are all essential setup for his OotP estrangement. Without any of this, his break with the family in OotP has no foundation in the films.

damaging_scenes.scene: OotP Film Scene 13 - Ministry corridor, Percy hurries past
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Percy is present at Harry's disciplinary hearing as court scribe, ignores his father and Harry completely, marches past 'his back rigid and his nose in the air,' and later laughs sycophantically at Fudge's joke about Harry. He is actively complicit in the Ministry's persecution of Harry.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Percy appears briefly in a corridor at the Ministry, is called by a young wizard ('They're waiting for your report downstairs'), and hurries off. Harry watches him go. There is no recognition, no interaction, no context for their estrangement.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The film reduces Percy's OotP role to a single wordless cameo. His active complicity in Harry's hearing, his sycophantic laugh at Fudge's joke, his ignoring of his father - all the specific behaviors that make his estrangement concrete and damning - are entirely absent.

damaging_scenes.scene: OotP Film - Absent: Percy's letter to Ron, the family estrangement
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Percy sends Ron a letter advising him to drop Harry and report to Umbridge, quotes himself in the Daily Prophet supporting Umbridge's appointment, and Ron tears the letter to pieces. Percy sent back his Christmas jumper without a note. Mrs. Weasley cries. The family is fractured.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Percy's estrangement from the family, his letter to Ron, his public support for Umbridge and Fudge, and the family's grief are entirely absent from the film.
damaging_scenes.explanation: This is the emotional core of Percy's arc - his betrayal of family for career, the specific cruelty of the letter, Mrs. Weasley's grief. Without it, his return in DH has no weight because the audience has not witnessed the estrangement that makes the reconciliation meaningful.

damaging_scenes.scene: DH Part 1 Film Scene 14 - Ministry atrium, Ron sees Percy
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: In the book, Percy is seen at the Ministry as part of Scrimgeour's entourage at Dumbledore's funeral, and Ron stabs his kipper 'with unwonted venom.' The estrangement is palpable.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Ron sees Percy in the Ministry atrium during the infiltration. Percy begins to open his mouth. Ron says 'Piss off.' Percy says nothing. They stare wordlessly.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Without the established estrangement arc, this moment lands as a generic hostile sibling encounter rather than the culmination of years of painful family fracture. The 'Piss off' is tonally right but contextually hollow for film audiences.

damaging_scenes.scene: DH Part 2 Film Scene 15 - Battle of Hogwarts, Percy's return
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Percy arrives through the tunnel, is stunned to find his family, calls himself 'a fool... an idiot... a pompous prat... a Ministry-loving, family-disowning, power-hungry moron' (Fred supplies the last), shakes Fred's hand, hugs his mother, apologizes to his father. Then fights alongside Fred, jokes with him for the first time in years, and is present when Fred dies.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Percy appears among the group emerging with Harry at Hogwarts (listed by Snape as one of those who appear). He participates in the battle. His reconciliation with the family is not shown as a distinct scene. His reunion moment is not dramatized.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Percy's return and reconciliation is one of the most emotionally resonant moments in DH, made devastating by Fred's death immediately after. Because the film never established the estrangement properly, the reconciliation cannot land. Percy's self-condemnation, Fred's forgiveness, and the subsequent death form a complete tragic arc that the film cannot deliver.
